clean_story: Light’s smallest piece.  Energy without mass. "Photon" comes from Greek phōs, meaning "light"—because that’s what they are: pure packets of light, carrying energy but no weight. We see photons as sunlight or laser beams, but they’re also the invisible messengers behind radio waves, X-rays, and the heat from a stove. Think of them like tiny, indestructible couriers—each one delivers a fixed "amount" of energy, depending on its color. A red photon carries half the energy of a blue one. This isn’t opinion; it’s measured in electronvolts. No matter how far they travel—a millimeter or a million light-years—they never slow down, never wear out. Here’s the strange part:Photons behave like both waves and particles. Drop one through two slits, and it creates an interference pattern, like ripples in water. Yet when detected, it lands as a single dot, like a bullet. This duality powers solar panels—photons knock electrons loose in silicon, creating electricity. In fiber optics, they bounce through glass cables at 200,000 km/s, carrying your internet. The fastest "thing" in the universe?A photon in a vacuum. Nothing outruns it. Three related subjects are wave-particle duality, electromagnetic spectrum and quantum entanglement.
